inbound.subject: Starting over after a layoff
inbound.body: Hi coach, I want to get back to running after a messy few months. My big goal is to get healthy enough to race a half marathon again this spring. Right now I mostly need help rebuilding without doing something stupid.

outbound.subject: Re: Starting over after a layoff
outbound.text:
Thanks for the message — I’m sorry the last few months have been messy. I hear you: the priority is rebuilding gradually and avoiding setbacks so you can be healthy for a spring half.
To get started I just need one key piece of info: your realistic time availability. Over a typical week, how many days can you commit to training and roughly how much time can you give per session (or total hours/week)? Also mention any days or times that are impossible for you to train.
Once I have that I’ll put together a gradual, conservative rebuild plan tailored to your schedule. Reply with your availability and I’ll take it from there.
— Coach

inbound.subject: Profile details and target race
inbound.body: Here is the missing context more directly. Primary goal: run a half marathon on 2026-05-17 and finish feeling strong. Time availability: 4 sessions per week and about 4 to 5 hours total. Experience level: intermediate. Constraints: mild Achilles tightness, busy work mornings on Tuesdays, and I prefer structured guidance.

outbound.subject: Re: Profile details and target race
outbound.text:
Got it — thanks. I’ve noted the race date (2026-05-17), your 4×/week availability (~4–5 hours total), mild Achilles tightness, and that Tuesday mornings are busy. Plan stays conservative: keep sessions controlled, protect the Achilles, and hit the key work without adding extra load.
Today
- Easy only — keep it very relaxed and monitor the Achilles closely. Shorten or stop if you feel any increase in sharp pain.
This week (4 sessions, ~4–5 h total)
1) Easy aerobic 1 — 45–60 minutes very easy. Conversational pace; focus on light cadence and a tall posture. (Avoid scheduling this in your Tuesday morning window.)
2) Easy aerobic 2 — 45–60 minutes easy. Keep the effort low; include a few short strides only if the Achilles feels fine.
3) Reduced-intensity session — 30–40 minutes with 1–2 blocks of steady effort (e.g., 2×8–10 minutes steady at comfortably-hard but controlled). If the Achilles is irritable, make this an easy aerobic session instead.
4) Strength — 30–40 minutes. Emphasize glute/hip strength, single-leg stability, and gentle calf eccentrics (no heavy plyometrics). Keep tempo controlled; stop any exercise that aggravates the Achilles.
Notes and priorities
- Execution: controlled and responsive. If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy or rest — don’t try to make up intensity later in the week.
- Technique cues: keep cadence light and posture tall throughout.
- Recovery: prioritize steady sleep, simple nutrition, and low stress during work mornings (short relaxation routines help).
- Plan-level: we’ll include periodic deloads (reduce weekly volume ~20%) as part of the build; you won’t need to add extra sessions to “catch up.”
Safety
- No hard sessions if the risk moves to red. If you notice increasing or sharp Achilles pain that doesn’t improve with downgrading, stop running and get it checked.
Do this week’s easy session and tell me how the Achilles feels — brief update after the first run is helpful.

inbound.subject: Availability update for this week
inbound.body: Availability update for this week. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate feels green. I can run Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday. Week is not chaotic. Schedule variability is medium because work may move one session by a few hours.

outbound.subject: Re: Availability update for this week
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Thanks for the availability update. With Achilles monitoring and a pain score of 2/10, and a green risk level, we’ll run a four-session week within your Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun window, totaling about 4–5 hours.
Monday — easy aerobic 45–60 minutes. Keep the pace easy, posture tall, cadence relaxed. Stop early if anything sharp or increasing.
Wednesday — easy aerobic 45–60 minutes. Same guardrails: easy pace, relaxed technique.
Friday — reduced-intensity 30–40 minutes with 1–2 steady blocks (for example 2 x 5–10 minutes at a steady effort). If the Achilles is irritated, convert to easy and avoid extra load. Do not try to recoup missed intensity later in the week.
Sunday — strength 30–40 minutes focusing on glutes/hips, single‑leg stability, and gentle calf eccentrics. Keep it controlled and smooth.
Safety and pacing notes:
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy.
- Do not make up missed intensity later in the week.
- Prioritize sleep and simple recovery habits to support the build.
- Avoid heavy plyometrics while the Achilles is cautious.
If you need to nudge a session by a few hours due to work, you can; just aim to complete all four sessions this week.
Let me know how it goes.
Coach

inbound.subject: First check-in after an easy run
inbound.body: Training check-in. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is green. I did a run for 45m and 6 km yesterday. Felt good, energy ok, little sore, slept well. Missed sessions count: 0. I want to keep building carefully.

outbound.subject: Re: First check-in after an easy run
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Nice work keeping things steady. You’re handling the build well with a mild Achilles signal (2/10) and green risk, which lets us proceed with a careful, solid week.
Today (Monday): easy_aerobic 45–60 minutes. Keep it light and conversational in pace, with a tall posture and relaxed cadence. If Achilles pain increases beyond 2/10 or you feel sharp or unusual pain, downgrade or stop.
Wednesday: easy_aerobic 45–60 minutes. Same approach—easy effort, smooth form, and attentive to the Achilles.
Friday: reduced-intensity 30–40 minutes with 1–2 steady blocks (about 7–10 minutes each) or convert to easy if irritation arises. Maintain controlled load and good technique.
Sunday: strength session 30–40 minutes focusing on glute/hip strength, single‑leg stability, and gentle calf eccentrics.
Key reminders:
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session and do not try to make up missed intensity later in the week.
- Prioritize sleep, hydration, and simple recovery habits to support the build.
- We are aiming for a conservative, setback‑avoidant progression toward the May 17 half marathon. This week’s plan stays within that guardrail.
Let me know how today goes.

inbound.subject: Long run felt fine
inbound.body: Long run update: I got through 75 minutes this morning and honestly I feel ok after the long run. The Achilles is there in the background but not angry, and I slept well last night. I'd like to keep the same four-day rhythm this week if that still makes sense.

outbound.subject: Re: Long run felt fine
outbound.text:
Nice work on the 75-minute run this morning. It’s good to hear you’re feeling okay after it and that the Achilles is there in the background but not angry, plus you slept well. Keeping the four-day rhythm this week fits your current constraints, so here’s the plan:
Four sessions this week (Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun)
- Monday – easy_aerobic, 45–60 minutes. Easy, conversational pace. Keep cadence light and posture tall. Monitor the Achilles and adjust if anything changes.
- Wednesday – easy_aerobic, 45–60 minutes. Same cues as Monday: easy effort, smooth form, watch discomfort levels.
- Friday – reduced-intensity, 30–40 minutes with 1–2 steady blocks of 7–10 minutes each. If irritation rises, convert to an easy effort for the whole session. No hard intervals or spikes in load.
- Sunday – strength, 30–40 minutes focusing on glute/hip work, single-leg stability, and gentle calf eccentrics. Keep movements controlled and expect light, steady effort.
Key guidance
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy. Do not try to make up missed intensity later in the week.
- Maintain light cadence and tall posture throughout all sessions. Prioritize sleep and simple recovery habits to support the week.
I’ll review Achilles response after this week’s easy sessions and adjust if needed. Let me know how it goes, especially after Wednesday.

inbound.subject: How easy should easy feel?
inbound.body: Quick question: on these comeback runs, how easy should easy actually feel? I'm trying not to chase pace, but I also don't want to jog so slowly that the mechanics get weird.

outbound.subject: Re: How easy should easy feel?
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Great question. On comeback runs, easy should feel truly easy—conversational and controlled. You’re not chasing pace; you’re rebuilding tolerance and protecting the Achilles.
How to gauge easy:
- Talk test: you can speak in full sentences.
- Effort: roughly 2–4 out of 10.
- Form cues: light cadence, tall posture, relaxed shoulders, and no overstriding.
If the Achilles shows irritation or sharp pain, drop the effort until it settles, and revisit the form cues.
For this week, keep the four-session rhythm and downgrade if you notice any uptick in pain or fatigue. No hard intervals or big spikes while risk remains elevated.
If you want to report after Wednesday’s easy run, that’s fine, but it’s optional.
Coach

inbound.subject: Work got messy
inbound.body: Can we adjust the week? Work is crazy and I probably only have Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday for training. I missed one session already and my stress is definitely up.

outbound.subject: Re: Work got messy
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Thanks for the update. I know work is chaotic and your stress is up—that’s exactly why we’ll keep things tight and conservative now. We’ll fit training into three sessions this week (Wednesday, Friday, Sunday) to protect consistency and avoid overdoing it while your availability is limited.
This week’s plan (three sessions):
- Wednesday — easy_aerobic, 45–60 minutes. Use the talk test; aim for effort around 2–4/10, keep a relaxed cadence, tall posture. If Achilles irritation occurs, downgrade to an even easier easy run.
- Friday — strength, 30–40 minutes. Focus on glute/hip strength, single‑leg stability, and gentle calf eccentrics. Keep movements controlled and light; stop or shorten if pain increases.
- Sunday — skills, 30–40 minutes. Technique work (cadence, posture, foot placement) with easy, precise movements. No hard intervals.
Key guardrails to protect progress:
- Energy is the limiting signal. Stay with the minimum effective dose and avoid hard effort.
- If energy drops, cut the session short; if energy rebounds, resume cautiously.
- Prioritize sleep, nutrition, and downshifting stress to support recovery.
Notes:
- Sessions can shift a few hours on the same day if work requires it, but we’ll keep the three-session structure.
- Optional: after Wednesday’s easy run, a brief update on how the Achilles feels is welcome.
Let me know how it goes after Wednesday, and we’ll adjust as needed.
Coach

inbound.subject: Here are the details you probably need
inbound.body: More detail for the adjustment: event date is 2026-05-17, days available this week are 3, pain score is still 2 out of 10, risk candidate feels yellow because work stress is high, week is chaotic true, stress score 8, sleep score 5.

outbound.subject: Re: Here are the details you probably need
outbound.text:
I hear you — work is hectic and sleep is low this week. I’ve tightened the plan so you keep fitness without adding stress: three controlled sessions (Wed/Fri/Sun), lower volume, and strict rules to back off if pain or fatigue rises.
Top points (read first)
- Keep every session easy, smooth, and technically tidy. Effort ~2–4/10, talk test intact.
- Do NOT add extra sessions or try to “make up” missed intensity later in the week.
- If Achilles pain or overall fatigue increases, downgrade or stop the session immediately (see specifics below).
This week’s sessions (do them on your available days)
- Wednesday — Easy aerobic run, 45–60 min
- Warm‑up 5–10 min easy jog + a few gentle drills (leg swings, high knees x20–30s each).
- Main: continuous easy running at talk‑test effort (~2–4/10). Focus on tall posture, quick light steps, slightly higher cadence, soft landings.
- Cool‑down 5–10 min easy jog/walk and light calf/glute mobility.
- Stop or convert to an easy walk/bike if Achilles pain becomes sharp or rises above your baseline; shorten to 20–30 min if energy is very low.
- Friday — Strength, 30–40 min (quality over load)
- Focus: glute and hip strength, single‑leg stability, gentle calf eccentrics.
- Example structure: 2–3 rounds of 3–4 exercises (single‑leg RDLs, split squats/step‑ups, glute bridges, 3x15 slow calf eccentrics). 8–12 reps per strength move, controlled tempo.
- Keep loads moderate; no heavy barbell drops, no plyometrics. If Achilles is sensitive, replace eccentrics with seated calf work or reduce range.
- Sunday — Skills / technique, 30–40 min
- Short warm‑up, then 20–30 min easy running focused on technique: small cadence increase (≈5–10%), upright posture, light foot strike and short ground contact.
- Use short, focused drills (3–4 x 20–30s) rather than long hard efforts. Finish with easy cooldown.
Weekly adjustments & rules
- Weekly volume: reduce roughly 20% from a normal week — this is intentional.
- If pain or fatigue increases meaningfully at any point, downgrade to an easy recovery session (walk, easy bike, or 20–30 min easy run) or rest.
- Do not chase lost intensity later in the week.
Recovery priorities (simple, high‑return actions)
- Aim to add whatever extra sleep you can (earlier bedtime, short nap) — even 30–60 extra minutes matters.
- Keep protein intake steady, hydrate, and do short mobility/soft tissue work after sessions.
Safety reminder
- No hard intervals, heavy plyos, or spikes this week. If your Achilles pain worsens or does not settle after downgrading, stop training and tell me immediately.
That’s the week — three focused, low‑stress sessions designed to keep you moving toward the May 17 goal without adding risk. Let me know how Wednesday feels (quick note after the run is perfect).

inbound.subject: Tune-up effort felt controlled
inbound.body: Small milestone: I did a tune-up 5k effort in 24:50 and it felt controlled. I added a 20m cool-down jog after. No big pain spike, just normal post-workout heaviness, and sleep was good.

outbound.subject: Re: Tune-up effort felt controlled
outbound.text:
Great to hear your tune-up felt controlled and there was no pain spike. Sleep appears solid too, which helps with recovery. I’m keeping your week simple and anchored on three days (Wed, Fri, Sun) to protect recovery and build consistency.
Today you have an optional short mobility or light recovery window if you feel tight or stiff.
Weekly plan (anchor sessions on Wed/Fri/Sun):
- Wednesday: 45–60 minutes easy aerobic run. Warm-up 5–10 minutes, cooldown 5–10 minutes. Pace: easy and conversational (talk test ~2–4/10). If Achilles irritation or sharp pain appears, drop effort or convert to a walk.
- Friday: 30–40 minutes of strength with a glute/hip focus and single‑leg stability, plus gentle calf eccentric work. No heavy loads or plyometrics. If the calf is sensitive, substitute seated calf work.
- Sunday: 30–40 minutes of skills/technique work. 20–30 minutes easy running emphasizing cadence and posture with short drills; no hard efforts.
Overall, keep weekly volume about 20% below normal and avoid adding extra sessions or higher intensity. Prioritize sleep, hydration, protein, and light mobility to support recovery. If Achilles pain worsens, stop or downgrade immediately.
If you want to share how Wednesday goes, I’ll be ready to adjust next steps. Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Still on the same four-day pattern
inbound.body: Just to confirm, I'm still doing four days per week most weeks. The same general Monday Wednesday Friday Sunday pattern still works, although Friday has become the easiest day for me to protect.

outbound.subject: Re: Still on the same four-day pattern
outbound.text:
Thanks for confirming the four-day pattern. Friday remains the easiest day to protect, and this week we’ll keep to three anchor sessions on Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday with about 20% less volume to protect recovery and the Achilles.
Wednesday: 45–60 minutes easy aerobic run (5–10 minutes warm-up, 5–10 minutes cool-down). Keep effort relaxed; talk test around 2–4/10. Maintain tidy running technique and a tall posture.
Friday: 30–40 minutes strength work (glute/hip focus, single‑leg stability, gentle calf eccentrics). No heavy loads or plyometrics. If calves or Achilles feel sensitive, substitute seated calf raises or shorten the session.
Sunday: 30–40 minutes skills/technique (20–30 minutes easy running focused on cadence and posture) with short drills; no hard efforts.
Safety and pacing notes: energy is the limiting signal. If energy drops further, cut the session short rather than pushing through. If you rebound tomorrow, resume with caution rather than trying to make up lost work. Prioritize sleep, hydration, protein, and light mobility. Given the Achilles considerations, avoid hard intervals or sharp increases in load, and stop or downgrade immediately if pain worsens.
If you want, feel free to share a quick wrap-up after Wednesday’s run so we can fine-tune for Thursday/Friday. Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Travel week coming up
inbound.body: Travel week heads-up: next week is rough. I only have Tuesday, Thursday, and Saturday open, and hotel treadmill plus a tiny gym are all I'll have. I'd love the simplest travel-friendly version of the plan.

outbound.subject: Re: Travel week coming up
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Travel week is coming up, and I hear you want something simple that travels well and fits the hotel gym. We’ll keep three light anchor sessions, no hard efforts, and no drama.
Session plan for next week (Tue, Thu, Sat)
- Tue — easy aerobic, 30–40 minutes
- 5–10 min warm-up
- 20–25 min easy aerobic (talk test 2–4/10)
- 5–10 min cool-down
- Keep effort relaxed and technique tidy
- If energy is low, cut a bit short (still stay moving)
- Thu — easy aerobic, 30–40 minutes
- same structure
- Sat — strength, 20–30 minutes
- Focus on glute/hip stability and gentle calf work
- No heavy loads or plyometrics
- Sample options if equipment is limited:
- Glute bridges, bodyweight squats or goblet squats with light hold
- Single-leg stability work (supported pistol-progressions or step-ups)
- Clamshells or side-lying leg lifts (bands if available)
- Gentle calf raises (seated if Achilles is sensitive)
- 2–3 sets of 8–12 reps (or 30–60 seconds per exercise if you’re using circuits)
- Stop or back off if pain or sharp discomfort occurs
Important notes:
- Energy is the limiter: keep every session easy, smooth, and technically tidy.
- If energy drops further, shorted session rather than forcing through.
- If tomorrow you rebound, resume with caution rather than trying to “make up” the work.
- Prioritize sleep, hydration, protein, and light mobility.
That keeps you moving through travel without stressing the Achilles or your recovery.
Safe travels, and we’ll regroup after you’re back.

inbound.subject: Travel details
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 3. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow because I am traveling for work. Week is chaotic true. Equipment access is treadmill and gym, no track. I can do 40m treadmill runs and one short strength session.

outbound.subject: Re: Travel details
outbound.text:
I hear you — travel and a chaotic week. Keep it simple and protective: three short, easy sessions that keep load low and monitor the Achilles.
Plan for the week (Tue / Thu / Sat):
Tue — Easy aerobic (treadmill) — 30–40 min total
- 5–10 min easy warm-up, 20–30 min steady easy running (talk test, RPE ~2–4/10), 5 min cool-down.
- Keep treadmill at ~0–1% incline, focus on relaxed posture and cadence. Stop or shorten immediately if pain increases above your baseline.
Thu — Short strength (hotel gym) — 20–30 min
- 2–3 sets of 8–12 reps or 30–60s per exercise. Pick controlled, low-load choices: single-leg RDL or step-ups, glute bridges, side-lying clams/hip abductions, seated calf raises (gentle). No heavy loads, no plyometrics. Prioritize quality and control over weight.
Sat — Skills / mobility session — 20–30 min
- Dynamic warm-up, mobility and form work. Options: easy treadmill cadence work (2–3 × 60 s slightly quicker turnover with full recovery), marching/technique drills if you have space, and light calf/ankle mobility. Keep everything sub‑maximal and relaxed.
Key priorities and safety
- Keep every session controlled and technically tidy. This is about consistency, not load spikes. 
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade to a shorter walk/easy jog or stop the session. Do not try to ‘‘make up’’ intensity later in the week. 
- Support recovery: prioritize sleep, simple nutrition (protein at meals), hydration, and brief mobility. 
Logistics
- No hard intervals or heavy/plyometric lifts while traveling. Keep effort smooth and respond to how the Achilles feels during the warm-up.
Let me know how the Tue treadmill run feels (especially the Achilles) and I’ll adjust the next block based on that.

inbound.subject: Hotel treadmill check-in
inbound.body: Travel check-in: I did a run for 40m on the hotel treadmill, about 4.5 miles, avg hr 146, and roughly 520 calories. Felt decent, slept ok, and nothing got worse. Mostly just mentally flat from travel.

outbound.subject: Re: Hotel treadmill check-in
outbound.text:
Nice work getting a 40-minute hotel treadmill session in. Travel can sap energy, and it’s good to hear you felt okay and nothing worsened.
Here’s the plan for the week, unchanged from our travel-friendly base guidance. The two anchor sessions stay (easy aerobic and short strength/mobility), with one additional light session if energy allows.
Tuesday — easy treadmill aerobic, 30–40 minutes
- 5–10 minute warm-up, 20–30 minute easy run, 5 minute cool-down
- Talk test; aim for RPE 2–4 / 10
- Treadmill incline 0–1%; tall, relaxed posture
Thursday — short strength/mobility, 20–30 minutes
- 2–3 sets of controlled exercises: single-leg RDL or step-ups, glute bridges, side-lying clams/hip abductions, seated calf raises
- Light loads, focus on form and control
- Stop or downgrade if anything feels off or painful
Saturday — skills/mobility, 20–30 minutes
- Dynamic warm-up and mobility work
- Optional 2–3 x 60 s cadence drills with full recovery at a light, comfortable pace
- Light calf/ankle mobility work
Key guardrails
- If Achilles pain increases, stop or downgrade immediately
- Prioritize sleep and hydration; keep recovery simple
- No hard intervals or high-load movements this week; keep load conservative
You’re maintaining the foundation for the May 17 event while staying travel-safe. Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Back home and Saturday is open
inbound.body: Good news: for the next month Saturday is open again, so I have more flexibility for the long run. Week to week I still want to stay at four days, but now the long run doesn't have to be Sunday every time.

outbound.subject: Re: Back home and Saturday is open
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Nice to have the long-run day flexible with Saturday open—that fits your four‑day plan while keeping the long run reachable. We’ll shift the long run to Saturday and keep the other sessions anchored to your four‑day pattern.
Plan for the week (four sessions):
- Tuesday: easy aerobic 30‑40 minutes. 5‑10 min warm‑up, 20‑30 min easy running (talk test, RPE 2‑4/10), 5‑10 min cool‑down. Focus on relaxed technique.
- Thursday: short strength and mobility 20‑30 minutes. 2‑3 sets of controlled single‑leg RDLs, step‑ups, glute bridges, clams, and seated calf raises with light loads. Keep it smooth and controlled.
- Saturday: long easy run 40‑60 minutes. Pace easy enough to talk. If energy allows, finish with 5‑10 minutes of light cadence drills; otherwise just cool down.
- Sunday: skills/mobility 20‑30 minutes. Dynamic warm‑up, 2‑3 x 60s light cadence drills, mobility work.
Notes to keep you on track:
- Energy is the limiting signal. Stay conservative and use the minimum effective dose to protect consistency.
- Achilles safety: avoid hard efforts. Stop or downgrade immediately if pain increases beyond baseline.
- Sleep, nutrition, and downshifting stress remain key. If energy drops further, cut the session short rather than forcing it; if you rebound tomorrow, resume cautiously.
That’s the week. If you want to adjust timing, you can shift within the four‑day framework, but this keeps your structure intact.
Coach

inbound.subject: Family chaos week
inbound.body: This week got blown up by family stuff. I only got in one 30m run and I'm feeling low energy today. Can we just simplify everything for a few days and keep the important things important?

outbound.subject: Re: Family chaos week
outbound.text:
Thanks for letting me know. It’s smart to simplify this week when energy is low and family stuff is in the way. We’ll keep it focused on the essentials and stay conservative with load.
Here’s the plan for the coming days (four-session week, with the option to pull back if needed):
- Tuesday — easy aerobic 30–40 minutes (5–10 min warm-up, 20–30 min easy running, 5–10 min cool-down). Use the talk test; aim for RPE around 2–4/10. 
- Thursday — short strength/mobility 20–30 minutes (2–3 sets of controlled, light work: single-leg RDLs, step-ups, glute bridges, clams, seated calf raises). Keep loads light and technique solid.
- Saturday — long easy run 40–60 minutes. Pace easy enough to talk; if energy allows, an optional 5–10 minutes of light cadence drills.
- Sunday — skills/mobility 20–30 minutes (dynamic warm-up, 2–3 x 60s light cadence drills, mobility work).
Today/this week action: you may opt for just a short mobility or recovery session if that’s all you have energy for. Any extra session beyond the four should stay easy and optional.
Important safety notes: we’re protecting the Achilles while you rebuild. Stop or downgrade any session if pain increases or sharp discomfort emerges. If energy remains low, we shorten the session rather than force it.
Two anchors to protect this week: ensure Tue easy aerobic and Thu strength/mobility happen as planned. Sat long easy run and Sun skills/mobility stay as optional additions if you’re feeling up to it.
If you want to shift times within these windows, that’s fine—the core is to keep the week alive with minimal, effective work and clear progression when your energy allows.
Wishing you steady recovery this week.

inbound.subject: Details for the family-chaos week
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 2. Pain score: 3 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow. Family stuff blew up this week. I only got in a 30m run. Felt low energy, sore, poor sleep. Stress score is 9. I am worried about losing momentum.

outbound.subject: Re: Details for the family-chaos week
outbound.text:
I hear you — family chaos, poor sleep and low energy this week. That’s why we’ll keep things very simple: protect consistency without forcing load so you don’t lose momentum or irritate the Achilles.
Plan for this week (2 brief sessions only):
- Session 1 — Easy aerobic run (anchor): 30–40 minutes total. 5–10 min very easy warm-up, 20–30 min conversational running (RPE ~2–4 / talk test), 5–10 min easy cooldown. Keep cadence light and posture tall. Monitor Achilles closely: if pain gets sharper or meaningfully worse, stop and switch to walk + mobility.
- Session 2 — Easy aerobic + recovery focus (anchor): 20–30 minutes. Option A: 20–30 min easy run at the same easy effort. Option B: 10–15 min easy run + 10–15 min mobility/short strength (bodyweight single-leg RDLs/step-ups/glute bridges, seated calf raises). No hard efforts, no plyometrics.
Execution notes (most important):
- Keep everything easy and controlled — this week’s goal is maintenance and tissue calm, not volume or intensity gains.
- If pain or fatigue rises, downgrade immediately to walking/mobility or stop the session. Do not try to make up missed intensity later in the week.
- Prioritize sleep and simple recovery: short naps if possible, consistent meals with protein, gentle movement, and easy hydration.
This approach protects your training consistency for the May 17 goal while letting you handle the family situation. Follow the two anchors and keep sessions short and easy. Let me know how your Achilles and energy feel after the second session.

inbound.subject: Back on track
inbound.body: Better week. I ran three times: 35m easy, 50m steady, and 80m today for about 9 miles. Felt good, slept well, and the Achilles feels quiet again. Same four-day rhythm still seems like the sweet spot.

outbound.subject: Re: Back on track
outbound.text:
Hi,
Nice to hear the Achilles is quiet again and you slept well—that's a good sign as you rebuild after the layoff. For this week we’ll stay conservative with two anchored sessions to protect tissue and keep you progressing.
Session 1 — 30–40 minutes easy aerobic
- 5–10 min easy warm-up
- 20–30 min conversational running (RPE 2–4 / talk test)
- 5–10 min cooldown
Session 2 — 20–30 minutes easy aerobic OR 10–15 minutes easy run + 10–15 minutes mobility/short strength
- Mobility/short strength options (if you choose the second option): bodyweight single-leg RDLs, step-ups, glute bridges, seated calf raises
- If you choose easy aerobic, keep it gentle with the same warm-up/cooldown structure
Key cautions and priorities
- No hard efforts this week; protect Achilles and overall energy
- If energy drops or pain increases, downgrade or stop immediately. Do not attempt to make up missed intensity later in the week
- Prioritize sleep, hydration, and simple recovery (light mobility, breathing, easy movement)
- Maintain a tall posture and light cadence during all runs
That’s the plan for the week—steady, controlled progress with a focus on consistency. Stay listening to your body and keep the tissue calm as you move forward.
Coach

inbound.subject: Should I add strides?
inbound.body: Question for you: since things are stable again, should I add strides after one easy run, or keep everything fully aerobic for now?

outbound.subject: Re: Should I add strides?
outbound.text:
Nice work—great to hear the Achilles is quiet and your energy is up.
If you want to reintroduce some turnover, you can add strides after an easy run, but only if you’re fully recovered from that run and not experiencing any new soreness.
How to do it (keep it conservative):
- After an easy run, add 4 x 15–20 m strides. Gradually accelerate to about 85–90% of max effort, then roll back to a relaxed jog or walk for 60–90 seconds between strides.
- Focus on smooth form and light, quick steps; no tempo or all-out surges.
- Keep the main run entirely aerobic; strides are a light turnover drill, not extra load.
- Stop immediately if you feel Achilles pain, sharp tightness, or a change in symptoms. If pain returns or worsens, skip strides and stay with easy aerobic this week.
Today your action remains optional short mobility or a light recovery session, not a hard workout.
Otherwise, keep the plan conservative for the week and stay aligned with the two-anchor structure until you’re clearly ready to progress.
Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Harder session left me cooked
inbound.body: Yesterday I did a harder session: total run 55m with 6 x 3 minutes moderate-hard. Today I'm pretty cooked, sleep was poor, and soreness is definitely up. I don't think anything is injured, but it feels like I pushed the edge.

outbound.subject: Re: Harder session left me cooked
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Nice work through yesterday’s harder session. With today’s fatigue, a poor night’s sleep, and higher soreness, we’ll keep this week conservative and protect the two anchor sessions.
Today: optional short mobility/recovery only.
- If you have energy, do a 15–20 minute mobility block (hips, calves, glutes, thoracic spine) followed by 10–15 minutes of easy aerobic work (easy jog or easy cycle) at an RPE of 2–3. Stop if anything sharp or new crops up.
Anchors for the week (non-negotiable):
- Anchor 1: Easy aerobic session (25–40 minutes, RPE 2–3). Fully aerobic; no pace goals.
- Anchor 2: Strength/mobility touch (20–30 minutes): gentle bodyweight strength plus mobility focusing on hips, glutes, and calves. Keep it light and controlled; avoid loads that stress the Achilles.
Cadence and posture: keep cadence light and posture tall; move with calm control.
Recovery basics: prioritize sleep, hydration, and simple, low-friction routines.
If the week tightens, keep only the anchors. Any extra sessions should stay easy and optional.
Optional stride option: if you’re feeling recovered from today or tomorrow’s easy work, you may add up to 4 x 15–20 m strides after an easy run, gradually increasing to 85–90% with 60–90 s jog/walk recoveries. Stop immediately if Achilles pain or new tightness appears; otherwise resume with easy aerobic only.
That’s the plan for now. The anchors stay the priority; tune only to your recovery signals and avoid pushing through fatigue.
Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Numbers from the hard session
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 5 out of 10. Risk candidate feels yellow. Intervals yesterday: run 55m total with 6 x 3 minutes moderate-hard. Felt exhausted after, soreness high, sleep poor. Pain affects form false. Pain worsening false. No swelling.

outbound.subject: Re: Numbers from the hard session
outbound.text:
Good call to be cautious — after that 6×3' effort and poor sleep you’re legitimately “cooked.” We’ll keep this week conservative and protect consistency rather than chasing any extra load.
Plan for this week (minimum effective dose)
- Today (recovery): 15–20 minutes gentle mobility and soft tissue work. Add 10–15 minutes very easy aerobic (walk/jog) only if you feel up to it. Keep effort conversational; stop immediately for any increase in Achilles or sharp pain.
- Anchor 1 — Easy aerobic (one session this week): 25–40 minutes, RPE ~2–3 (you should be able to talk easily). Keep cadence light and posture tall; prioritize relaxed mechanics over pace.
- Anchor 2 — Light strength + mobility (one session this week): 20–30 minutes. Focus on single-leg balance, glute/hip activation, and controlled calf loading (no heavy plyos). Keep it slow and controlled.
- Optional secondary session(s): If energy allows, a single reduced-intensity easy run 20–30 minutes. Otherwise skip. Overall weekly volume: reduced ~20% from your normal plan this week (deload).
- Strides: only if completely pain-free and clearly recovered — up to 4×15–20 m after an easy run, accelerating to ~85–90% with 60–90 s recovery. Stop immediately for any Achilles tightness or new pain.
Priorities and safety rules
- Energy is the limiting signal today — cut any session short rather than forcing completion. If you feel better tomorrow, resume cautiously; don’t try to “make up” hard work.
- No hard intervals or high-load spikes this week. Keep everything easy and controlled while soreness and poor sleep resolve.
- Focus on sleep, nutrition, and downshifting stress — those are the fastest levers to recover tissue and energy.
Start with the mobility block today and keep all runs easy. Let me know how you feel after the recovery work and the easy aerobic anchor so I can tweak the next couple of sessions.

inbound.subject: Only three days next week
inbound.body: Next week I only have three training days because of work travel again. I'd like the week simplified and I want to protect the most important session, but keep the overall direction the same.

outbound.subject: Re: Only three days next week
outbound.text:
Hi there,
I know travel is squeezing training again. We’ll keep it simple: three days next week, with two easy anchor sessions and one light strength session, preserving the overall base direction and protecting the most important work.
Planned sessions
- Session 1: Easy aerobic, 25–40 minutes at an easy effort (RPE 2–3). Keep it smooth and technically tidy.
- Session 2: Light strength/mobility, 20–30 minutes. Focus on posture, hips, and control.
- Session 3: Easy aerobic, 25–40 minutes at an easy effort (RPE 2–3).
Energy and safety notes
- If energy drops further, cut the session short rather than forcing completion.
- If energy rebounds tomorrow, resume with caution rather than trying to make up missed work.
- Strides remain optional only if completely pain-free and after an easy run; stop immediately if any Achilles pain increases.
- Prioritize sleep, nutrition, and overall stress downshift to support recovery.
You’re set for a lean, safe week that keeps you on track for the base phase. Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Three-day week details
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 3. Pain score: 3 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow. Next week I only have three training days. Week is chaotic false, but volume has to come down. Please simplify the week and protect the key session.

outbound.subject: Re: Three-day week details
outbound.text:
Got it — three training days next week, pain 3/10, risk yellow. Keep it simple, protect the one key session, and don’t add volume. Today: easy only and monitor the Achilles.
Plan for the week (three sessions)
- Session 1 — Easy aerobic (25–40 min): steady, conversational pace (RPE ~2–3/10). Focus on smooth mechanics and relaxed breathing. Keep it genuinely easy — if pain or unusual tightness appears, back off or stop.
- Session 2 — Light strength & mobility (20–30 min): bodyweight and low-load exercises (single-leg balance, glute bridges, hip hinge, split squats, calf mobility + gentle eccentric/calves rehab if part of your program). Move deliberately; this is tissue-support work, not max effort.
- Session 3 (key session) — Easy aerobic (35–40 min if you can): make this the protected anchor. Same effort (RPE ~2–3), keep it steady and technically tidy. If you need to shorten it, do so rather than forcing extra minutes.
Optional strides: only after an easy run, only if completely pain-free. Keep to 3–4 x 15–20 m at ~85–90% with full recovery; stop immediately if the Achilles or any new pain increases.
Rules to follow this week
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully at any point, downgrade the session to easy or end it early. Do not try to make up missed intensity later in the week. 
- No hard intervals or high-load plyometrics while this risk remains yellow. 
- Prioritize steady sleep, simple nutrition (protein + carbs after sessions), and low-stress recovery habits to support tissue response.
Why this works: shorter, controlled sessions keep stimulus while reducing total load and protecting the Achilles. The middle strength session supports mechanics and durability so your key aerobic anchor stays meaningful.
Keep it simple and conservative this week — protect the key aerobic run, monitor pain closely, and cut anything that doesn’t feel right. Let me know how the anchor session goes and the Achilles response.

inbound.subject: Race confidence check
inbound.body: With the race getting closer, do you think the current plan still fits where I am? I'm curious what the biggest focus should be over the next two weeks, especially since I've settled into that four-day pattern pretty well.

outbound.subject: Re: Race confidence check
outbound.text:
Yes — the current base plan still fits. With the race approaching, the top priority for the next two weeks is consistency plus tissue protection: keep the four weekly sessions, keep them easy and technically tidy, and protect the key aerobic anchor rather than chasing pace or extra volume.
This week (and for the next two weeks) follow the validated four-day skeleton:
- Tue — Easy aerobic: 25–40 min, RPE ~2–3 (conversational). Focus on smooth mechanics and relaxed breathing. Stop or shorten the session if energy or Achilles pain increases. 
- Thu — Strength: 20–30 min, low-load, quality-focused (single-leg balance, glute bridges, hip hinge, split squats, calf mobility and gentle eccentrics). Keep tempo controlled; don’t push to failure. 
- Sat — Key anchor easy aerobic: 40–60 min protected. Keep effort fully aerobic (RPE 2–3); this is the session we prioritize. Optional 3–4 x 15–20 m strides only if you feel completely pain-free and relaxed — stop immediately for any Achilles change. 
- Sun — Skills / easy recovery: 20–30 min easy with running drills and mobility, or an active recovery walk/run if you’re tired. Keep technique tidy; no hard reps.
Immediate coaching rules to follow:
- Energy is the limiting signal: cut the session short rather than forcing completion if you feel flat. 
- If Achilles or any pain rises above baseline, stop and downgrade; do not add hard work. 
- If you rebound the next day, resume cautiously — don’t try to “make up” missed work. 
- Prioritize sleep, simple post-session nutrition, and downshifting stressors this week so the sessions land.
Today’s instruction: do the planned session but conservative — keep it shorter or easier if needed. Let me know how the Saturday anchor feels.

inbound.subject: Mild cold and weather
inbound.body: I have a mild cold and the weather is awful this week. Energy is not great. I don't want to do anything dumb, but I also don't want to lose the thread completely. How conservative should I be?

outbound.subject: Re: Mild cold and weather
outbound.text:
Got it — mild cold, low energy, and awful weather. Good call to be conservative. Here’s a safe, simple way to keep the thread without risking a setback.
This week: follow the usual Tue/Thu/Sat/Sun skeleton but scale everything back and keep intensity very low. Priorities: rest if you have systemic symptoms, protect the Achilles, and keep all sessions conversational and short when needed.
Plan (examples you can choose from depending on how you feel):
- Tue — Easy aerobic 20–30 min. Options: treadmill walk/jog, easy indoor bike, or a brisk walk. RPE ~2–3 (conversational). No strides. Stop if energy or symptoms drop.
- Thu — Light strength & mobility 15–25 min. Bodyweight, single-leg balance, glute bridges, hip hinge, gentle calf mobility/eccentrics. Low load, high quality.
- Sat — Protected anchor 30–40 min easy aerobic if you feel up to it. Keep this fully aerobic (RPE 2–3). If energy is low, reduce to 20–30 min or replace with a long walk. No strides or harder work while you’re sick.
- Sun — Skills/recovery 15–20 min easy: drills, mobility, short easy jog or walk.
Safety rules — stop or escalate immediately if any of these occur:
- Fever, chills, widespread muscle aches, rapid heartbeat, shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, or lightheadedness → stop all training and seek medical advice.
- Symptoms move beyond a mild "above-the-neck" cold (new or worsening cough, difficulty breathing, worsening fatigue) → rest and contact a clinician.
- If Achilles pain increases above baseline or changes quality → stop the session, downgrade activity, and don’t attempt strides/plyos.
If you only have mild nasal symptoms and no fever, light activity as above is reasonable — but cut sessions short and stop immediately if you feel worse.
Practical tips: prioritize sleep and fluids, eat a small mix of carbs+protein after sessions, keep layers on in bad weather, and prefer indoor options to avoid exposure.
Do this conservative week and let me know right away if you develop any red flags or if the Achilles changes. If everything stays mild, we’ll resume the normal skeleton next week and keep all sessions easy until you’re fully recovered.

inbound.subject: Illness details
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 3. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow. Recent illness is mild. Energy score is 4. Sleep score is 5. I want to know how conservative I should be this week.

outbound.subject: Re: Illness details
outbound.text:
Short answer: be conservative — keep every session easy, shorten if you feel low on energy, and stop or downgrade immediately if pain or symptoms rise.
Given your recent mild illness, energy 4/10, sleep 5/10 and pain 2/10 (risk = yellow), follow the validated three-session skeleton but err to the low end of durations and intensity.
Planned sessions (do these across 3 available days):
- Easy aerobic — 20–30 minutes, RPE 2–3 (conversational). Easy warm-up (5 min walk/jog + mobility), smooth mechanics, steady breathing. Stop or turn into a walk if Achilles or fatigue worsens.
- Strength & mobility — 20–25 minutes, low load, quality reps only. Focus: single-leg balance, glute bridges, hip hinge/split-squat progressions, calf mobility and gentle eccentrics only if pain is stable and very low. Keep it controlled; no heavy loading or plyometrics.
- Anchor easy aerobic — 30–45 minutes fully aerobic at RPE 2–3. Given low energy and recent illness, favor 30 min if you need to conserve. No strides this week unless you feel completely normal, pain-free (0/10), and fully recovered — otherwise skip.
Key rules to follow this week:
- If pain or fatigue increases meaningfully, downgrade the session to an easy walk/mobility or stop. Do not force missed intensity later in the week.
- Keep effort smooth and technically tidy; prioritize tissue protection over pace or time.
- Prioritize sleep, simple post-session nutrition, and hydration to aid recovery.
- If systemic symptoms return (fever, worsening cough, severe fatigue), stop training and rest; escalate to a clinician if needed.
Bottom line: train, but conservatively. Shorter sessions at lower effort, clear stop/downgrade rules, and no hard work or strides unless you feel fully recovered. Let me know how the first session feels and any change in your Achilles or overall energy.
outbound.html:
Short answer: be conservative — keep every session easy, shorten if you feel low on energy, and stop or downgrade immediately if pain or symptoms rise.

Given your recent mild illness, energy 4/10, sleep 5/10 and pain 2/10 (risk = yellow), follow the validated three-session skeleton but err to the low end of durations and intensity.

Planned sessions (do these across 3 available days):
- Easy aerobic — 20–30 minutes, RPE 2–3 (conversational). Easy warm-up (5 min walk/jog + mobility), smooth mechanics, steady breathing. Stop or turn into a walk if Achilles or fatigue worsens.
- Strength & mobility — 20–25 minutes, low load, quality reps only. Focus: single-leg balance, glute bridges, hip hinge/split-squat progressions, calf mobility and gentle eccentrics only if pain is stable and very low. Keep it controlled; no heavy loading or plyometrics.
- Anchor easy aerobic — 30–45 minutes fully aerobic at RPE 2–3. Given low energy and recent illness, favor 30 min if you need to conserve. No strides this week unless you feel completely normal, pain-free (0/10), and fully recovered — otherwise skip.

Key rules to follow this week:
- If pain or fatigue increases meaningfully, downgrade the session to an easy walk/mobility or stop. Do not force missed intensity later in the week.
- Keep effort smooth and technically tidy; prioritize tissue protection over pace or time.
- Prioritize sleep, simple post-session nutrition, and hydration to aid recovery.
- If systemic symptoms return (fever, worsening cough, severe fatigue), stop training and rest; escalate to a clinician if needed.

Bottom line: train, but conservatively. Shorter sessions at lower effort, clear stop/downgrade rules, and no hard work or strides unless you feel fully recovered. Let me know how the first session feels and any change in your Achilles or overall energy.

inbound.subject: Half marathon done
inbound.body: Race update: I ran the half marathon today in 1:55. I jogged 15m after, I feel tired but happy, and nothing feels scary. This whole build really confirmed that four days per week is a good groove for me.

outbound.subject: Re: Half marathon done
outbound.text:
Nice work finishing the half today—1:55 is solid, and it’s great to hear four days per week feels sustainable for you.
Given your current energy and the race fatigue, we’ll keep this week light and conservative with three easy, tissue-friendly sessions to protect consistency and recovery:
Session 1 — Easy Aerobic (20–30 min total)
- 5 min easy warm-up, then 15–20 min of easy running at RPE 2–3 (conversational), finish with 5 min easy cooldown or walk.
- Stop or walk if Achilles pain or fatigue worsens. Cadence stays light and posture tall.
Session 2 — Strength & Mobility (20–25 min)
- Low-load, quality reps only: single-leg balance, glute bridges, hip hinge/split-squat progressions, calf mobility with gentle eccentrics if pain is stable.
- Downgrade or skip any exercise that aggravates pain beyond baseline.
Session 3 — Anchor Easy Aerobic (30–45 min, aim ~30 min)
- Fully aerobic pace; keep effort easy. If you feel any Achilles issues, shorten accordingly.
- Strides are not needed this week and should only be added if you are completely recovered with 0/10 pain.
Key guardrails this week
- If energy stays low, shorten any session further or skip nonessential parts; do not try to “make up” missed work later.
- If energy rebounds tomorrow, resume with caution rather than trying to re-create last week’s volume.
- Prioritize sleep, fueling, and downshifting overall stress to support tissue health.
You’re doing the right thing by preserving consistency. We’ll reassess after this week and adjust as needed. Train smart. Coach

inbound.subject: Next-goal question
inbound.body: Now that the race is done, what should the next two weeks look like and how should I think about the next goal? I'm open to another half or maybe building toward something longer later in the year.

outbound.subject: Re: Next-goal question
outbound.text:
Great work on getting the half done — that’s a solid milestone. You said you’re tired but happy; that’s exactly the place to be: conservative and intentional for the next two weeks so we protect tissue and rebuild consistency.
Plan — next two weeks
Week 1 (this immediate post-race week) — recovery first (3-session minimal dose)
- Session 1: Easy aerobic 20–30 min (5 min warm-up, 15–20 min easy). RPE 2–3, conversational. Keep cadence relaxed.
- Session 2: Strength & mobility 20–25 min (low-load: single-leg balance, glute activation, ankle mobility, core stability). Quality over load; no heavy lifts or plyos.
- Session 3 (anchor): Easy aerobic 30–45 min (favor ~30 min if energy is low). Fully aerobic — easy and steady. No strides this week.
- Daily: optional short mobility/recovery touch only. Prioritize sleep, hydration, and simple recovery routines.
- If Achilles pain increases above baseline or new sharp pain appears, stop and downgrade to walk/mobility immediately.
Week 2 — re-establish the four-day groove (gentle)
- Tue: Easy aerobic 25–40 min, conversational (RPE 2–3).
- Thu: Light strength & mobility 20–30 min (add one or two low-load strength sets if feeling good; still avoid heavy loading).
- Sat: Protected anchor easy aerobic 40–60 min (fully aerobic; favor 40 min to restart). Keep effort smooth and technically tidy. Only consider very short pickups or strides after several easy runs and only if Achilles = 0/10.
- Sun: Recovery 20–30 min easy run or walk + mobility.
- Rule of thumb: protect the two anchors (one aerobic anchor + one strength/mobility). If the week gets tighter, keep only those anchors. Any extra sessions stay easy and optional.
How to think about the next goal
- Another half soon: after these two weeks, you can progress into a 6–8 week half-specific block if you want a relatively quick follow-up. That block should start with steady aerobic weeks, add one quality session (tempo/threshold) in week 3–4 only if Achilles and energy are fully settled, and raise the long run gradually.
- Building toward something longer later in the year: focus on consistency first — re-establish the four-day rhythm and build base volume slowly over 12+ weeks before adding race-specific work. That gives time to raise aerobic capacity and protect tissues.
Safety and priorities (short list)
- No hard sessions or strides until you’re fully recovered and Achilles pain = 0/10. This week: skip strides.
- Protect the anchors: they keep the week alive. If you must cut sessions, keep those.
- Keep everything easy, relaxed, and technically tidy; sleep and simple recovery habits matter more than tempo right now.
That’s the plan — conservative now, then steady progress. Let me know how the anchor session in Week 1 feels and whether the Achilles settles; we’ll pick the right pathway to a half or a longer build from there.

inbound.subject: One more durable schedule note
inbound.body: One thing I learned from this block: four days is sustainable for me, and Saturday is probably the best long-run anchor going forward. Fridays still need to stay pretty light because of work.

outbound.subject: Re: One more durable schedule note
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Nice job listening to what your body is telling you. Four days a week feels sustainable for you, and Saturday as the long-run anchor makes sense. Fridays staying light due to work is noted. Here’s your recovery-week plan, kept conservative and tissue-friendly:
- Tuesday — Easy aerobic: 25–40 minutes at a conversational pace (RPE 2–3). Keep it relaxed, smooth, and fully aerobic.
- Thursday — Strength & mobility: 20–30 minutes of low-load work focusing on technique and mobility. Prioritize quality over quantity; no pain.
- Saturday — Anchor easy aerobic: 40–60 minutes, fully aerobic. If restarting, lean toward the shorter end.
- Sunday — Skills/recovery: 20–30 minutes, or an easy recovery run/walk.
Important: no strides this week. If energy drops, shorten the session rather than pushing through. If energy rebounds tomorrow, resume with caution rather than trying to make up missed work. Prioritize sleep, fueling, and downshifting overall stress.
Fridays stay light as you noted; use that day for mobility or easy rest if possible.
This plan aligns with your post-race guidance: protect anchors, stay at easy effort, and build consistency first. After this two-week block, we’ll reassess Achilles status and energy to decide on the next phase.
If you notice any increase in Achilles pain or overall fatigue, adjust immediately.
Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Freeform final check-in
inbound.body: Quick final check-in before I go quiet for a few days: easy 35m jog today, about 4 miles, avg hr 138, felt good, slept well, and I'm mostly just happily tired. Curious if your advice changes much given everything you've seen from me over this stretch.

outbound.subject: Re: Freeform final check-in
outbound.text:
Nice work today—sounds like you’re happily tired and slept well. Based on your update, no changes to the plan are needed. You’re in a conservative recovery week and we’ll keep the four-day groove when you’re online again, with two anchor easy sessions and light work on the other days, protecting the Achilles and recovery.
This week’s structure (unchanged):
- Tue: easy aerobic 25–40 minutes (conversation pace)
- Thu: strength & mobility 20–30 minutes (low-load, focus on posture and technique)
- Sat: anchor easy aerobic 40–60 minutes (shorter if restarting; around 30–40 minutes acceptable)
- Sun: skills/recovery 20–30 minutes or easy recovery run/walk
- Fri: light day due to work; mobility or very easy recovery
If the week tightens for any reason, keep only the anchor sessions. Any additional session should be easy and optional. Key priorities remain: tissue protection, sleep, hydration, and simple routines.
Safety note: No hard sessions while risk is elevated; no strides this recovery week; stop or downgrade if Achilles pain increases.
Post-race focus: we’ll reassess Achilles and overall energy after the two-week recovery-to-groove block to plan next phase.
Safe offline time. When you’re back, we’ll pick up from this plan. Optional: let me know how it goes.
